questions.stem: Gross domestic product (GDP) is best defined as the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders over what period?
options: The entire history of the country
An indefinite period with no fixed length
A given period, usually one year
questions.explanation: GDP is a flow measured over a defined period, normally a year or a quarter. It captures the market value of all final goods and services produced inside the country's borders during that period.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: GDP measured by the expenditure approach is the sum of consumption, investment, government spending, and ___.
questions.explanation: By the expenditure approach $GDP = C + I + G + (X - M)$, where the final term is net exports, that is exports minus imports.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Why does national income accounting count only final goods and services rather than every transaction?
options: Because intermediate goods have no value
To avoid double counting the value of intermediate goods
To exclude services from the total
Because final goods are cheaper to measure
questions.explanation: The value of intermediate goods is already embodied in the price of the final good. Counting both would double count, so only final goods and services enter GDP. An equivalent fix is the value added method.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The main difference between gross national product (GNP) and gross domestic product (GDP) is that GNP includes what?
options: Net income earned by residents from abroad
questions.explanation: GNP equals GDP plus net factor income from abroad (income residents earn overseas minus income foreigners earn domestically). GDP is location based, GNP is ownership based.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Nominal GDP differs from real GDP because nominal GDP is measured using what prices?
options: Current prices of the year in question
Prices from a fixed base year
questions.explanation: Nominal GDP uses current prices, so it rises when either output or prices rise. Real GDP holds prices at a base year, isolating changes in the quantity of output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If nominal GDP is 600 and the GDP deflator is 120 (base year = 100), what is real GDP?
questions.explanation: Real GDP = nominal GDP divided by the deflator, times 100. So real GDP = $600 / 120 \times 100 = 500$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The GDP deflator is calculated as which ratio?
options: Nominal GDP divided by real GDP, times 100
Real GDP divided by nominal GDP, times 100
questions.explanation: The GDP deflator is $(\text{nominal GDP} / \text{real GDP}) \times 100$. It is a broad price index covering all goods and services in GDP.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the simple circular flow of income, households supply factors of production to firms and in return receive what?
options: Factor incomes such as wages, rent, interest, and profit
questions.explanation: Firms pay households for the use of labour, land, capital, and enterprise. These factor incomes (wages, rent, interest, profit) flow back to households, who then spend them on goods and services.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the circular flow model, which of the following is a withdrawal (leakage) from the flow of income?
questions.explanation: Saving, taxation, and imports are withdrawals because they take income out of the domestic circular flow. Investment, government spending, and exports are injections that add to it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following counts as an injection into the circular flow of income?
questions.explanation: Injections add spending to the domestic economy and consist of investment, government spending, and exports. Saving, taxes, and imports are withdrawals.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The three approaches to measuring national income (output, income, and expenditure) should in principle give what result?
options: The same total, because one person's spending is another's income
A total that is always largest for the income method
A total that doubles with each method
questions.explanation: The output, income, and expenditure methods measure the same flow from three angles. What is spent on output becomes income to factor owners, so the three totals are equal apart from statistical discrepancies.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Net domestic product (NDP) is obtained by subtracting what from gross domestic product?
questions.explanation: NDP = GDP minus depreciation. Depreciation, also called capital consumption, is the value of capital used up in production during the period.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of these transactions is included in GDP?
options: The resale of a used car
A newly built house sold this year
A gift of money from a parent to a child
The sale of shares on the stock market
questions.explanation: GDP counts current production of final goods and services. A newly built house is current output. Second hand sales, pure money gifts (transfers), and financial asset trades do not represent new production.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Why are government transfer payments such as pensions and unemployment benefits excluded from GDP?
options: They are not payments for current production of goods or services
They are always too small to matter
They are counted twice in consumption
questions.explanation: Transfer payments move income from one group to another without any good or service being produced in return. Since GDP measures production, transfers are excluded to avoid overstating output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A rise in real GDP per capita is most commonly used as an indicator of what?
options: The size of the government budget
A change in average living standards
questions.explanation: Real GDP per capita divides real output by population, giving average output per person. It is a standard proxy for material living standards, though it ignores distribution and non market activity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a recognised limitation of using GDP as a measure of welfare?
options: It always falls when output rises
It ignores unpaid work and the value of leisure
It double counts all final goods
It is measured in physical units rather than money
questions.explanation: GDP omits household and voluntary work, leisure, environmental costs, and the informal economy. These gaps mean GDP is an imperfect guide to overall wellbeing.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The value added method measures a firm's contribution to GDP as the value of its output minus what?
options: The cost of intermediate goods bought from other firms
questions.explanation: Value added equals output value minus the cost of intermediate inputs bought in. Summing value added across all firms gives GDP without double counting.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In an economy with no government and no foreign trade, GDP by the expenditure method equals which sum?
questions.explanation: With no public sector and no trade, the terms $G$ and $(X - M)$ vanish, leaving $GDP = C + I$, consumption plus investment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a country's exports are 90 and its imports are 110, the net exports component of GDP is:
questions.explanation: Net exports = exports minus imports = $90 - 110 = -20$. A trade deficit makes the net export term negative, reducing GDP.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Personal disposable income is the income households have available for spending and saving after deducting what?
options: Direct taxes and adding transfer payments
questions.explanation: Disposable income equals personal income minus direct taxes plus transfer payments received. It is the amount households can actually choose to consume or save.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'I' in the expenditure identity $GDP = C + I + G + (X - M)$ refers chiefly to what?
options: Household purchases of shares and bonds
Spending by firms on capital goods and changes in stocks
questions.explanation: In national accounts, investment means gross fixed capital formation (new plant, machinery, buildings) plus the change in inventories. It is not the everyday sense of buying financial assets.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Comparing GDP across countries using a common currency and adjusting for differences in price levels uses which concept?
questions.explanation: Purchasing power parity (PPP) converts incomes using the relative cost of a common basket of goods, so comparisons reflect what money can actually buy in each country rather than market exchange rates alone.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Activity in the informal or 'black' economy tends to cause official GDP figures to do what?
options: Understate the true level of economic activity
Overstate the true level of economic activity
questions.explanation: Unrecorded and hidden transactions are missed by official statistics, so measured GDP is lower than actual production. This is one reason GDP understates true activity in many economies.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If real GDP grows by 4 percent while the population grows by 4 percent over the same year, real GDP per capita will roughly do what?
questions.explanation: Real GDP per capita grows at approximately the growth of output minus the growth of population. With both at 4 percent, the difference is about zero, so output per person is roughly unchanged.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which statement about a 'stock' versus a 'flow' in national accounting is correct?
options: GDP is a stock, while national wealth is a flow
GDP is a flow, while national wealth is a stock
Both GDP and national wealth are stocks
Both GDP and national wealth are flows
questions.explanation: A flow is measured per unit of time, while a stock is measured at a point in time. GDP (output per year) is a flow, whereas the nation's accumulated wealth is a stock.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When the income method is used to measure national income, which of the following should NOT be included?
options: Transfer payments such as state pensions
questions.explanation: The income method sums factor incomes: wages, rent, interest, and profit. Transfer payments are not payments for productive services, so including them would overstate national income.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
